sub_questions.question: Who originally claimed that the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels?
sub_questions.answer: The Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) made the original claim.

sources.title: Iran says Strait of Hormuz closed to U.S.-Israel-allied ships | Myanmar International TV
sources.quote: Iran’s Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) announced on Friday that passage is prohibited for any vessel through any corridor in the Strait of Hormuz to and from the ports of countries allied with and supporting US and Israel.

sub_questions.question: What is the precise meaning of 'effectively closed' in this context (e.g., physical blockade, threat, advisory)?
sub_questions.answer: 'Effectively closed' refers to the prohibition of passage for US/Israel-linked vessels, enforced by the IRGC through turning back ships, warnings, and threats of severe consequences. An Iranian official later clarified it meant ships would require Iranian authorization and toll payment before transiting.

sources.title: Iran says Strait of Hormuz closed to U.S.-Israel-allied ships | Myanmar International TV
sources.quote: Besides, the IRGC Navy declared the Strait of Hormuz is closed, warning that any transit will face severe consequences according to the statement.

sources.title: Reopening the Strait of Hormuz is easier said than done. Here's why | CBC
sources.quote: Ebrahim Azizi, head of the Iranian parliament's National Security Commission, said Saturday the strait was 'returning to the status quo.' He said earlier that this means ships would require Iranian authorization and toll payment before transiting.

sub_questions.question: When was this claim first made and what was the initial context or source?
sub_questions.answer: The claim was first made by the IRGC on March 27, 2026, in retaliation for an air war launched by the US and Israel against Iran and the assassination of its supreme leader.

sources.title: Iran says Strait of Hormuz closed to U.S.-Israel-allied ships | Myanmar International TV
sources.quote: Iran’s Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) announced on Friday that passage is prohibited for any vessel through any corridor in the Strait of Hormuz to and from the ports of countries allied with and supporting US and Israel.

sub_questions.question: Are there any official statements or reports from US, Israeli, or international maritime authorities confirming or denying restrictions on vessels in the Strait of Hormuz?
sub_questions.answer: The US responded by announcing its own naval blockade of Iranian ports starting April 13, 2026, and clarified that this blockade would not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting to/from non-Iranian ports. The International Maritime Organization (IMO) strongly rejected any unlawful tolls or restrictions, emphasizing freedom of navigation as a fundamental principle of international maritime law.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz closure - Wikipedia
sources.quote: April 12: U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) announced that US forces would begin implementing a blockade of 'all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports' starting April 13 at 10 a.m. Eastern Time. CENTCOM clarified that this blockade would be enforced 'impartially' against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports, but 'will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports.'

sources.title: International Maritime Organization statements
sources.quote: The IMO stressed that freedom of navigation is a fundamental principle of international maritime law and must be respected by all parties. They reject any imposition of tolls, fees, or discriminatory transit measures for passage through international straits.

attribution_chain.description: Tweet by @currentreport1 claiming 'Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels'
Myanmar International TV (myanmaritv.com) reporting IRGC announcement
CBC News reporting on the situation, referencing Iranian officials and US actions
Web search timeline summarizing US official statements and actions
International Maritime Organization (IMO) statements on freedom of navigation and concerns

supporting_evidence.quote: Iran’s Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) announced on Friday that passage is prohibited for any vessel through any corridor in the Strait of Hormuz to and from the ports of countries allied with and supporting US and Israel.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Directly confirms Iran's claim to close the Strait to specific vessels.

supporting_evidence.quote: It's the latest twist in a saga of uncertainty about the viability of the critical waterway, whose effective closure since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran on Feb. 28 has debilitated global trade and sent oil prices soaring.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Corroborates the effective closure and its impact on global trade.

contradicting_evidence.quote: April 12: U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) announced that US forces would begin implementing a blockade of 'all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports' starting April 13 at 10 a.m. Eastern Time. CENTCOM clarified that this blockade would be enforced 'impartially' against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports, but 'will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports.'
contradicting_evidence.relevance: The US blockade specifically targeted Iranian ports, implying that transit for non-Iranian related vessels to non-Iranian ports was not necessarily impeded by the US.

contradicting_evidence.quote: March 9: President Trump falsely claimed Iran's military had been destroyed and the Strait had reopened, stating the US would take control of it.
contradicting_evidence.relevance: Trump's claim of reopening, even if false, indicates a conflicting narrative and desire to counter the Iranian claim.

contradicting_evidence.quote: The IMO stressed that freedom of navigation is a fundamental principle of international maritime law and must be respected by all parties. They reject any imposition of tolls, fees, or discriminatory transit measures for passage through international straits.
contradicting_evidence.relevance: The IMO's rejection of tolls and discriminatory measures directly contradicts Iran's asserted right to close or restrict the Strait.

summary: The claim that the Strait of Hormuz was 'effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels' is partially confirmed. The Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) announced and attempted to enforce such a closure starting March 27, 2026, in retaliation for US/Israel actions, leading to practical disruptions and unsafe conditions. However, the US responded with its own naval blockade targeting Iranian ports, while clarifying it would not impede freedom of navigation for other vessels. International bodies like the IMO unequivocally rejected Iran's actions as unlawful and a violation of international maritime law, indicating that the 'closure' was not universally recognized or legitimate.
key_finding: While Iran declared and attempted to enforce a closure of the Strait of Hormuz to US/Israel-linked vessels, the US implemented a counter-blockade targeting Iranian ports, and international maritime authorities rejected the legitimacy of Iran's actions, creating a complex and contested situation rather than a universally recognized closure.
